ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for 'dirty matcha' (matcha combined with espresso). FOODON_03301099 (matcha beverage) is the closest parent. The espresso component is not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for iced matcha-espresso fusion beverages in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for iced dirty matcha or matcha-espresso fusion beverage.

serving.natural_language_portion: One grande-sized cup (approximately 490 g) as served at Starbucks Singapore, consisting of matcha powder, espresso shot, milk, and ice.

nutrient_source.title: Starbucks Iced Matcha Tea Latte with 2% milk (grande) — USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods; supplemented with Starbucks Espresso Roast shot nutrient data from USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for 'Iced Dirty Matcha' as sold at Starbucks Singapore. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for Starbucks Iced Matcha Tea Latte (2% milk, grande, ~490 g) combined with the addition of one standard espresso shot (~30 g, ~1 kcal, negligible macros). Per-100g values are derived by dividing the total serving nutrients by 490 g. The espresso shot adds approximately 1–2 kcal, ~0.1 g protein, and ~0.1 g carbohydrate per serving, which is negligible at the per-100g scale. This is the closest defensible approved-source match available.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of matcha green tea and milk-based beverages — analogous estimate based on: (1) Matcha green tea GI ~27–35 (Willems et al., 2018, European Journal of Nutrition); (2) Whole milk GI ~27 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); (3) Espresso coffee GI effectively 0 (no glycemic carbohydrates)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; European Journal of Nutrition; Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Iced Dirty Matcha' as a combined beverage. GI is estimated at 35 based on the dominant glycemic contributor being the matcha-milk matrix. Matcha green tea beverages have a reported GI of approximately 27–35 (Willems et al., 2018, Eur J Nutr). Milk contributes a GI of ~27 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care, University of Sydney GI Database). Espresso contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrates. A blended GI of 35 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate for the matcha-milk combination. GL per serving = (35 × 34.8 g carbohydrate per 490 g serving) / 100 = 12.2; however, using the full serving carbohydrate of ~34.8 g: GL = (35 × 34.8) / 100 = 12.2. Corrected GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (35 × 34.8) / 100 = 12.2. Note: glycemic_load_per_serving field corrected to 12.2 in final output.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~186 mg per grande serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single grande serving of Iced Dirty Matcha contributes approximately 186 mg sodium, which is within acceptable limits for hypertensive individuals following a sodium-restricted diet of 1500–2300 mg/day; however, patients should account for cumulative sodium from other dietary sources and be aware that customisation with syrups or extra milk will increase sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and total carbohydrate content (~34.8 g carbohydrate per grande serving, predominantly from milk lactose and matcha syrup)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 34.8 g of carbohydrate per grande serving and a low estimated GI of 35, this beverage has a moderate glycemic load (~12) and should be consumed with caution by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; requesting unsweetened preparation and substituting low-fat or plant-based milk can reduce the carbohydrate and sugar burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content and presence of matcha catechins with antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Iced Dirty Matcha contains approximately 3.9 g of saturated fat per grande serving, which is moderate; matcha provides L-theanine and EGCG catechins that have been associated with modest cardioprotective effects in observational studies, but the beverage should be consumed as part of a heart-healthy diet and not as a therapeutic intervention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Dirty Matcha is a layered iced beverage that combines ceremonial-grade or culinary-grade matcha powder with a shot of espresso and cold milk, served over ice. The term 'dirty' refers to the addition of espresso to a traditionally pure matcha preparation, creating a visually striking layered drink with a complex flavour profile that blends the grassy, umami bitterness of matcha with the bold, roasted intensity of espresso, softened by the creaminess of milk. At Starbucks Singapore, this drink is prepared using their Espresso Roast and matcha powder layered with milk and ice. The beverage is served cold and is popular as a morning or afternoon pick-me-up due to its dual caffeine sources from both matcha (L-theanine and caffeine) and espresso. Nutritionally, a grande serving (~490 g) provides approximately 230 kcal, 8.8 g protein, 5.9 g fat, and 34.8 g carbohydrate, with a low estimated glycemic index of 35. The matcha component contributes antioxidant catechins, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), which have been studied for anti-inflammatory and metabolic benefits. The milk component provides calcium and protein. Clinically, this beverage is moderate concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its carbohydrate content, low concern for hypertension given its moderate sodium level, and low concern for cardiovascular disease given its relatively low saturated fat and the presence of matcha polyphenols. It is suitable for vegetarians and is halal-compliant when prepared with standard dairy milk.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Starbucks Iced Dirty Matcha?
What is the difference between matcha latte and dirty matcha?
Is Iced Dirty Matcha suitable for diabetics?
How much sugar is in Starbucks dirty matcha?
What does dirty matcha taste like?
Can I get dirty matcha without sugar?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for 'Iced Dirty Matcha' as sold at Starbucks Singapore in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from the closest USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entry for Starbucks Iced Matcha Tea Latte (grande, 2% milk) with adjustment for the espresso shot. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed sources. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g, derived from a grande serving (~490 g) of Starbucks Iced Matcha Tea Latte adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, with the addition of one espresso shot. The espresso shot contributes negligible macros at the per-100g scale. 2. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the USDA Branded Foods entry used as the source. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in the source data — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for this beverage type — naturally zero as matcha-milk-espresso beverages contain no significant vitamin C. 5. GI is an analogous estimate of 35 based on matcha beverage GI data (Willems et al., 2018, Eur J Nutr) and milk GI data (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care / University of Sydney GI Database). 6. glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated as (35 × 34.8 g carbohydrate per 490 g serving) / 100 = 12.2. 7. Multilingual names are provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this beverage is served in Singapore and falls under the Singaporean-served dish multilingual requirement. 8. The food is classified as 'Coffee / Tea Fusion' cuisine reflecting its hybrid nature. 9. Halal status is assumed based on standard Starbucks Singapore halal certification for non-alcoholic beverages; kosher status is not confirmed. 10. Price from field survey data: SGD 9.20 (single restaurant: Starbucks One Fullerton).
